dashboard.ultimateGameDesc: Ready to test everything you've learned? Step into the ultimate energy challenge and see if you have what it takes.

courses.title: Grid Academy
courses.subtitle: Master the fundamentals of energy.
courses.course1_tk1_text: Electricity is created at power generating facilities using various fuel sources, including coal, nuclear energy, natural gas, and renewable resources like wind and solar.
courses.course1_tk2_text: Once generated, electricity moves across a network of high-voltage transmission lines, which act as the backbone of the electric grid to transport power over long distances.
courses.course1_tk3_text: Electricity arrives at a neighborhood substation, where the voltage is lowered to a safe, usable level. From there, it travels along the poles and wires in your community directly to your home's power outlet.
courses.course2_title: How Home Solar Energy Systems Operate
courses.course2_tk1_text: Solar panels, or solar PV, use semiconducting material to absorb sunlight and convert it into electricity (0:00-0:11).
courses.course2_tk2_text: The electricity produced is direct current (DC), which must be converted to alternating current (AC) via an inverter to be safe and usable for home appliances (0:17-0:30).
courses.course2_tk3_text: A meter installed in your home allows you to track real-time electricity generation (0:33-0:37).
courses.course2_tk4_text: Generation is weather-dependent; panels produce less on dull days and do not work at night. Because of this, homeowners typically remain connected to the National Grid.
courses.course2_tk5_text: While a solar battery can store excess energy, it usually isn't enough to make a home entirely self-sufficient. Any unused or unstored electricity is exported back to the grid.
courses.course3_tk1_text: The primary role is to raise or lower voltage levels. Electricity traveling at 735,000 volts must be stepped down to 120 or 240 volts for safe household use (0:57 - 1:12).
courses.course3_tk2_text: Substations direct electricity along the power grid and protect equipment through automated systems (0:43 - 0:48).
courses.course3_tk3_text: The "heart" of the substation, responsible for changing the voltage (2:34 - 2:43).
courses.course3_tk4_text: These act as safety mechanisms that can cut power automatically during a fault or manually for maintenance (1:50 - 2:31).
courses.course3_tk5_text: Critical safety devices that protect against over-voltage, such as lightning strikes, by directing excess electricity into the ground (2:50 - 3:12).
courses.course4_title: Can 100% of the World Run on Solar?
courses.course4_tk1_text: When photons strike silicon, they dislodge electrons, creating an electric current due to the electric field created at the PN junction between N-type and P-type silicon layers.
courses.course4_tk2_text: Solar energy is inconsistent based on geography and weather. Moving electricity and developing efficient storage are major logistical challenges. Most commercial systems are only 15–20% efficient.
courses.course4_tk3_text: Powering the world with solar technology is physically possible. Costs are decreasing, and innovations like floating solar farms are emerging. It already provides a safer alternative to kerosene in off-grid regions.

course2.q1_o2: Because they need a meter to monitor generation

course3.q0: What is the primary role of a substation?
course3.q0_o1: To raise or lower voltage levels

course4.q0: What happens when photons strike silicon in a solar cell?
